content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Most of us visit museums the way we read under a clock: quickly, dutifully, trying to see everything and remembering almost nothing. Nicole Junkermann has come to believe in the opposite approach, which she calls quiet looking. See less. Look longer. Let one painting or one object hold your attention completely, and let the rest go. This note is about visiting museums slowly, and about how galleries and reading enrich each other when both are taken at a gentle pace.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Nicole Junkermann on visiting slowly, looking deeply, and letting art and reading enrich each other.Quiet looking is the visual cousin of unhurried reading. Both ask you to resist the urge to consume and to choose depth instead. A museum visited slowly leaves you with a few works you genuinely saw, which is worth far more than a hundred you walked past while looking for the next room. The aim is not coverage. The aim is the kind of looking that stays with you.
The trouble with seeing everything
Great museums are overwhelming by design, and the instinct to see it all is understandable. But attention does not work that way. After an hour of trying to absorb everything, the eyes glaze and the mind goes quiet in the wrong way. You leave tired and oddly empty, having seen much and taken in little. The rooms blur together, and the one thing that might have moved you is lost in the crowd of things you barely registered.
The alternative is to choose. Pick a single gallery, or even a handful of works, and give them real time. The world’s great collections are vast, as anyone who has wandered the Metropolitan Museum of Art or a national gallery knows, and no one sees them whole in a day. Accepting that frees you to see a few things properly, which is the only way anyone ever really sees art at all.
How to look slowly
Quiet looking is a simple practice, but it takes a little discipline at first because it runs against the museum crowd flowing past. A few gentle habits help:

• Choose a few works in advance, or wander until one stops you, then stay with it.

• Stand in front of a single piece for several minutes, longer than feels natural.

• Look before you read the label, so the work speaks first and the caption second.

• Sit down when you can; the benches in galleries are there for exactly this.
The act of staying with one work is where the magic is. In the first few seconds you see the obvious. In the second minute you start to notice: the brushwork, the light, the small strange detail in the corner. By the third minute the painting has become a place rather than an image. This is the looking that lasts, and it cannot be rushed.
See less, look longer, and let one work hold you completely.
Museums and reading, hand in hand
Museums and books feed each other in the most natural way. A painting sends you to a book about the painter; a book about a place sends you to the museum that holds its art. Many great institutions now share large parts of their collections online and publish wonderful background reading. Exploring the British Museum or a national gallery website before or after a visit deepens both the looking and the reading. The post on reading while travelling touches on the same loop.
Nicole Junkermann often pairs a museum visit with a relevant book, reading a little before going and a little after returning. The book gives the art a context; the art gives the book a face. Together they hold a subject in the mind far better than either could alone, and the memory of the visit lasts much longer for it.
Smaller museums and quiet hours
The grandest museums are not always the best for quiet looking. Small and specialist collections, house museums, regional galleries and single artist studios are often calmer, less crowded, and easier to take in at a gentle pace. Nicole Junkermann seeks these out especially, finding that a modest museum visited slowly can move her more than a vast one visited in a rush. There is room to think, room to sit, and rarely a queue between you and the art.
Timing matters as much as choice. The first hour after opening and the last hour before closing are usually the quietest, when the rooms are nearly empty and the light is often at its best. A weekday is calmer than a weekend, and a grey day calmer than a bright one. Plan a visit for these quiet hours and even a popular gallery offers the stillness that real looking needs.
The quiet afternoon at the gallery
Put it all together and a museum becomes a place for a slow, restoring afternoon rather than a tick on a list. Arrive without rushing, see a little deeply, sit on a bench and let it settle, perhaps with a book in your bag for the cafe afterward. It is much the same spirit as a calm corner for reading, simply moved to a grander room full of other people’s masterpieces.
So next time you visit a gallery, resist the urge to see it all. Choose a few works, look at them properly, sit down, and let one of them change your afternoon. Bring a book, read around what you saw, and let the art and the reading talk to each other. Nicole Junkermann is sure you will remember one slowly seen painting long after a hundred glimpsed ones have faded.
